case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: User-provided full protocol with 15 min batch time, 83% isolated yield, openai council benchmark.

streams.reasoning: From Chemistry Plan stream_logic: phenylacetylene as dipolarophile kept separate from nitroacetate to prevent premature nitrile oxide dimerization. Equal flow rates with stream B at 2× concentration gives correct 1:2 stoichiometry.

From Chemistry Plan stream_logic: ethyl nitroacetate at 2× concentration of stream A to achieve 2.0 equiv stoichiometry with equal flow rates. Higher concentration compensates for incomplete nitrile oxide generation efficiency.

pre_council_proposal.mixing_order_reasoning: From Chemistry Plan: Rapid mixing at T-mixer essential before heated coil entry. Nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and prone to dimerization - immediate availability of dipolarophile upon generation prevents furoxan formation.
pre_reactor_steps: Pre-heat feed lines to 50-60°C to ensure DES remains fluid for pumping
post_reactor_steps: Cool product stream to room temperature
Collect for offline aqueous workup and extraction
pre_council_proposal.chemistry_notes: Thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition driven by 120°C heating in viscous DES medium. Rate-limiting step is nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate dehydration. Flow advantages: precise temperature control, rapid uniform heating in narrow tubing, controlled mixing prevents nitrile oxide dimerization. DES viscosity (~50-100 cP) requires wider tubing ID (1.0 mm) to prevent excessive backpressure. Calculations show mass-transfer limitation (Da=39) - T-mixer critical for rapid mixing. BPR required due to water elimination during nitrile oxide formation.

reasoning_per_field.residence_time_min: Calculation estimated 0.8 min (range 0.5-2.2 min) based on 20× intensification factor from batch 15 min. Analogy 2 used 60 min but for different mechanism - our thermal cycloaddition much faster.
reasoning_per_field.flow_rate_mL_min: Calculation: 0.5 mL/min total (0.25 + 0.25) to achieve 0.8 min residence time with 0.4 mL reactor volume. Analogy 2 used 0.5 mL/min for similar thermal process.
reasoning_per_field.temperature_C: Same as batch 120°C - Chemistry Plan specifies this temperature required for nitrile oxide generation. Analogy 1 used 200°C but for photochemical cascade, Analogy 3 used 154°C for different energetic compound.
reasoning_per_field.concentration_M: Mixed stream concentration 0.33 M (average of 0.5 M and 1.0 M streams). Lower than batch 0.5 M but compensated by better mixing and heat transfer in flow.
reasoning_per_field.BPR_bar: Calculation required 5.0 bar due to water elimination during nitrile oxide formation creating gas-liquid system. Analogy 3 used 47.6 bar but for much higher temperature (154°C).
reasoning_per_field.tubing_material: PFA chosen for 120°C temperature resistance and chemical compatibility with DES. Not photochemical so FEP transparency not needed. Analogy 1 used steel but for 200°C.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_ID_mm: 1.0 mm to manage DES viscosity and prevent excessive backpressure while maintaining good heat transfer. Calculation showed acceptable pressure drop. Analogy 2 used 1.2 mm for similar viscous system.
reasoning_per_field.reactor_volume_mL: 0.4 mL calculated from residence time × flow rate. Smaller than Analogy 1 (30 mL) due to much shorter residence time needed.

literature_analogies: Analogy 3: energetic compound synthesis at high temperature

chemistry_plan.reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
chemistry_plan.bond_formed: C-O and C-C bonds in isoxazole ring
chemistry_plan.bond_broken: N-O bond rearrangement in nitrile oxide generation; loss of H2O from ethyl nitroacetate

reagents: phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv, 0.5 M in TBAB/EG 1:5 DES)
feed_streams.reasoning: Substrate (dipolarophile) dissolved in DES; kept separate from nitroacetate to control mixing and prevent premature nitrile oxide dimerization

reagents: ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv, 1.0 M in TBAB/EG 1:5 DES)
feed_streams.reasoning: Nitrile oxide precursor dissolved in DES; 2.0 equiv used to drive reaction to completion

reagents.name: phenylacetylene
reagents.notes: Terminal alkyne; reacts regioselectively with nitrile oxide to give 5-phenylisoxazole

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide in situ at 120°C via dehydration; excess used to ensure full conversion

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.notes: Forms deep eutectic solvent with ethylene glycol; acts as reaction medium promoting the cycloaddition

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.notes: Hydrogen bond donor in DES; may facilitate nitrile oxide generation

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal dehydration/tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide (ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide, EtO2C-CNO) in situ. The nitro group undergoes aci-nitro tautomerism followed by elimination of water.

Concerted [3+2] 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide with phenylacetylene. The nitrile oxide (C≡N⁺-O⁻) acts as the 1,3-dipole and the terminal alkyne as the dipolarophile, forming the isoxazole ring regioselectively (5-phenyl regioisomer).

stream_logic.reasoning: Dipolarophile in DES; separated from nitrile oxide precursor to ensure controlled mixing at the T-mixer and minimize nitrile oxide dimerization before encountering the alkyne
Nitrile oxide precursor in DES; 2.0 equiv concentration to match batch stoichiometry via flow rate ratio (e.g., equal flow rates with Stream B at 2× concentration of Stream A)

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate should be mixed rapidly at a T-mixer just before entering the heated coil reactor. The nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and prone to dimerization (furoxan formation); immediate availability of the dipolarophile upon nitrile oxide generation is essential. Rapid mixing in a T-piece followed by immediate entry into the 120°C zone ensures the nitrile oxide is trapped by phenylacetylene before dimerization occurs.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species; the reaction proceeds under air in batch.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: No quench needed inline; the batch protocol quenches with water only for workup/extraction purposes, not to stop a dangerous reaction. In flow, the product stream can be collected and worked up offline.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: nitrile oxide generation from nitroacetate at elevated temperature is well-documented. The DES viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) may cause significant backpressure in narrow-bore tubing — a wider ID coil (e.g., 1.0-1.6 mm) is recommended. Residence time of 15 min at 120°C should translate directly to flow. Uncertain whether the DES fully dissolves both reagents at the required concentrations at room temperature for pumping — may need to pre-heat feed lines to ~50-60°C. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate suggests incomplete nitrile oxide generation efficiency; some may decompose or dimerize.

### Overall Kinetics Assessment

The kinetics landscape for the 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition candidates shows a range of residence times (τ) from 2 to 6 minutes. The literature suggests a τ_lit of 60 minutes for batch processes, with an intensification factor (IF) of 10× for thermal reactions, leading to a target τ of 6 minutes for flow. Candidates 25 and 26 meet this target, while candidates 1 and 27 propose shorter τ values, risking incomplete conversion. Conversion (X) is high for all candidates, but τ/τ_k margins are thin for candidates with τ = 2 min, posing RTD risks. The Chief should note that while candidates 25 and 26 are well-aligned with literature and IF expectations, candidates 1 and 27 require careful consideration of RTD effects and potential conversion shortfalls. ### Selection Rationale

Candidate 74 is selected with a combined score of 0.777, driven by its highest scores in chemistry (0.880) and kinetics (1.000), indicating a strong performance in reaction efficiency and rate. Runner-up 17 scored 0.758, held back by lower fluidics (0.550) and geometry (0.638) scores. Candidate 20 scored 0.743, with a lower chemistry score (0.750) affecting its overall performance. The user objective of a balanced approach favored candidate 74's strong chemistry and kinetics. Remaining uncertainties include the flow enhancement factor, which should be verified experimentally. The first bench experiment should verify the reaction kinetics under flow conditions to confirm the expected enhancement factor.

proposal.mixing_order_reasoning: From Chemistry Plan: Rapid mixing at T-mixer essential before heated coil entry. Nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and prone to dimerization - immediate availability of dipolarophile upon generation prevents furoxan formation.
pre_reactor_steps: Pre-heat feed lines to 50-60°C to ensure DES remains fluid for pumping
post_reactor_steps: Cool product stream to room temperature
Collect for offline aqueous workup and extraction
proposal.chemistry_notes: Thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition driven by 120°C heating in viscous DES medium. Rate-limiting step is nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate dehydration. Flow advantages: precise temperature control, rapid uniform heating in narrow tubing, controlled mixing prevents nitrile oxide dimerization. DES viscosity (~50-100 cP) requires wider tubing ID (1.0 mm) to prevent excessive backpressure. Calculations show mass-transfer limitation (Da=39) - T-mixer critical for rapid mixing. BPR required due to water elimination during nitrile oxide formation.

reasoning_per_field.residence_time_min: Calculation estimated 0.8 min (range 0.5-2.2 min) based on 20× intensification factor from batch 15 min. Analogy 2 used 60 min but for different mechanism - our thermal cycloaddition much faster.
reasoning_per_field.flow_rate_mL_min: Calculation: 0.5 mL/min total (0.25 + 0.25) to achieve 0.8 min residence time with 0.4 mL reactor volume. Analogy 2 used 0.5 mL/min for similar thermal process.
reasoning_per_field.temperature_C: Same as batch 120°C - Chemistry Plan specifies this temperature required for nitrile oxide generation. Analogy 1 used 200°C but for photochemical cascade, Analogy 3 used 154°C for different energetic compound.

case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: User-provided full protocol with 15 min batch time, 83% isolated yield, openai council benchmark.

The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal dehydration/tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide (ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide, EtO2C-CNO) in situ. The nitro group undergoes aci-nitro tautomerism followed by elimination of water.

Concerted [3+2] 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide with phenylacetylene. The nitrile oxide (C≡N⁺-O⁻) acts as the 1,3-dipole and the terminal alkyne as the dipolarophile, forming the isoxazole ring regioselectively (5-phenyl regioisomer).

stream_logic.reasoning: Dipolarophile in DES; separated from nitrile oxide precursor to ensure controlled mixing at the T-mixer and minimize nitrile oxide dimerization before encountering the alkyne
Nitrile oxide precursor in DES; 2.0 equiv concentration to match batch stoichiometry via flow rate ratio (e.g., equal flow rates with Stream B at 2× concentration of Stream A)

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate should be mixed rapidly at a T-mixer just before entering the heated coil reactor. The nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and prone to dimerization (furoxan formation); immediate availability of the dipolarophile upon nitrile oxide generation is essential. Rapid mixing in a T-piece followed by immediate entry into the 120°C zone ensures the nitrile oxide is trapped by phenylacetylene before dimerization occurs.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species; the reaction proceeds under air in batch.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: No quench needed inline; the batch protocol quenches with water only for workup/extraction purposes, not to stop a dangerous reaction. In flow, the product stream can be collected and worked up offline.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: nitrile oxide generation from nitroacetate at elevated temperature is well-documented. The DES viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) may cause significant backpressure in narrow-bore tubing — a wider ID coil (e.g., 1.0-1.6 mm) is recommended. Residence time of 15 min at 120°C should translate directly to flow. Uncertain whether the DES fully dissolves both reagents at the required concentrations at room temperature for pumping — may need to pre-heat feed lines to ~50-60°C. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate suggests incomplete nitrile oxide generation efficiency; some may decompose or dimerize.

analogies.summary: This photocycloaddition reaction utilizes triplet sensitization with 2,2'-dimethoxythioxanthone (2,2'-MeOTX) as the photocatalyst at 1 mol% loading under 455 nm COB LED irradiation. The flow process operates in acetonitrile at 200°C with a 5-minute residence time and 6.0 mL/min flow rate using a FlowSyn steel coil reactor (30 mL volume) equipped with a 34 bar back-pressure regulator. Flow chemistry enabled daisy-chaining of the initial photochemical step with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions, dramatically improving yield from 16% in batch (2 hours) to 88% in flow with a 24-fold intensification factor. The high-temperature operation required for the thermal cascade was much safer to implement in the continuous flow reactor compared to batch processing.

analogies.summary: This base-catalyzed nucleophilic ring-opening reaction was conducted in a cyclic continuous-flow reactor with 1.2 mm internal diameter tubing at 60°C using EtOAc as solvent. The flow process achieved 96% yield with a 60-minute residence time at 0.5 mL/min flow rate, compared to 93% yield requiring 20 hours in batch mode. The cyclic continuous-flow setup enabled a dramatic 20-fold reduction in reaction time (from 20 hours to 1 hour) while improving yield by 3 percentage points. The flow advantage likely stems from enhanced mass transfer and more consistent reaction conditions in the continuous system compared to the prolonged batch heating required for completion.

analogies.record_id: Yang 등 - 2024 - Safety Guided Process Development and Scale-Up of the Highly Energetic Compound 4-Methyl-1,2,5-oxadi.pdf
analogies.summary: This process involves the synthesis of 4-methyl-1,2,5-oxadiazole, a highly energetic compound, using a nitration or cyclization reaction in a concentrated sulfuric acid/water mixture (63:6.3 ratio). The flow process was conducted in a tubular reactor at 154°C with a residence time of 179 minutes (approximately 3 hours) and a flow rate of 0.12 mL/min, requiring a back-pressure regulator at 47.6 bar to maintain liquid phase conditions. Flow chemistry enabled a significant temperature increase from 65°C in batch to 154°C, resulting in an improved yield from 70.8% to 78.8%. The primary advantage of flow over batch was enhanced safety when handling this highly energetic compound, as the continuous process allows for better control of the exothermic reaction and reduces the inventory of hazardous material at any given time. The high pressure and temperature conditions achievable in the flow reactor also contributed to the improved reaction efficiency.

proposal.chemistry_notes: Thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition driven by 120°C heating in viscous DES medium. Rate-limiting step is nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate dehydration. Flow advantages: precise temperature control, rapid uniform heating in narrow tubing, controlled mixing prevents nitrile oxide dimerization. DES viscosity (~50-100 cP) requires wider tubing ID (1.0 mm) to prevent excessive backpressure. Calculations show mass-transfer limitation (Da=39) - T-mixer critical for rapid mixing. BPR required due to water elimination during nitrile oxide formation.
